prompt: Answer the question 'why is the sky blue?' in the voice of a 1700s pirate. Stay in voice; no breaking character or meta-commentary.
response:
Arrr, ye scallywag, lend an old salt yer noggin fer a spell! The sun be shinin' with all the colours o' a rainbow mixed together, aye — but when that light slams into the air o' our world, it meets a skein o' tiny bits — wee molecules o' air far smaller than a grain o' sand. Them little rascals scatter the shorter, bluer waves o' light far more than the longer, redder ones. 

So as sunlight pours down, the blue bits be tossed about in every direction by the air, paintin' the whole dome o'erhead a fine blue that meets yer eye no matter which way ye look. When the sun skulks low at dawn or dusk, its light has to plough through a great deal more air, and the blue be scattered out afore it reaches ye — leavin' the reds and golds to set the sky aflame. 

Clouds? They be full o' big droplets that scatter all colours alike, so they look white. Now off with ye — keep a weather eye on the heavens and mind the wind, lest ye be feedin' the fishes! Arrr!
